title: Motivational interviewing: explore ambivalence
vignette: A 52-year-old man comes to the office for a routine follow-up appointment. The patient has a history of well-controlled hypertension and hyperlipidemia. He has a 35-pack-year smoking history and reports that he continues to smoke about 10-15 cigarettes daily. Family history is significant for lung cancer in his mother. His father died following a stroke at age 65. When asked about whether he has considered quitting, the patient replies that he is not ready to stop smoking. He says, "I know I probably should, but I'm just too stressed at work to give up my cigarettes right now. To be honest, I'm probably smoking closer to a pack a day." Which of the following is the most appropriate response to the patient?

clues.text: continues to smoke about 10-15 cigarettes daily
clues.note: He is an active smoker - the target health behavior. The counseling task is to move him toward change, but only in a way matched to his readiness.

clues.text: he is not ready to stop smoking
clues.note: He is NOT ready to quit (precontemplation/contemplation). For a patient not ready to change, the evidence-based approach is motivational interviewing - explore ambivalence rather than push advice or facts.

clues.text: I know I probably should, but I'm just too stressed at work
clues.note: This is classic AMBIVALENCE: he acknowledges he 'should' quit but cites a barrier. The best response explores both sides of that ambivalence (reasons to quit vs not quit) to elicit his own motivation ('change talk').

whatMatters: Strip the story to: a smoker who is NOT READY to quit but is ambivalent ('I know I should, but...'). The best response uses MOTIVATIONAL INTERVIEWING - an open-ended, nonjudgmental question that explores his ambivalence about quitting vs not quitting, building his own internal motivation. That is option C. Giving advice/benefits (A, E) or reassurance (B) is premature.
strategy: For a patient NOT ready to change, the right answer is almost always the MOTIVATIONAL INTERVIEWING move: open-ended, nonjudgmental, exploring the patient's own ambivalence and reasons - NOT giving advice, reassurance, or facts yet. Pick the response that asks the patient to voice their own pros and cons.

options.text: "I suggest that you consider how your health may improve if you were to stop smoking."
"I understand that you're under a lot of stress, but you are capable of more than you think."
"What do you worry will happen if you quit smoking? What about if you don't quit?"
"What is your understanding of how smoking relates to lung cancer and stroke?"
"With all the smoking cessation treatment options available, quitting is a lot more manageable."

eliminations.label: unsolicited advice (your pick)
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: directive advice pushing the benefits of quitting ('consider how your health may improve'). ❌ WHY IT FAILS: it advances the agenda of quitting while ignoring his stated resistance/ambivalence; advice-giving to a not-ready patient tends to increase resistance. The trap is 'be a good doctor and recommend quitting,' but the timing is wrong. ✅ Correct if: the patient were already ready/committed and asking for guidance.

eliminations.label: reassurance, skips ambivalence
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: reassurance about his ability ('you are capable of more than you think'). ❌ WHY IT FAILS: although supportive, it neither explores his ambivalence nor elicits his own reasons, and can feel dismissive of his reluctance. ✅ Correct if: the goal were encouragement after he had already decided to quit.

eliminations.label: risk education, premature
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: an open question, but aimed at testing his knowledge of smoking-related risks. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: it shifts to teaching facts rather than eliciting his motivation; risk education is not the first motivational-interviewing step for a not-ready patient. ✅ Correct if: the patient lacked basic risk knowledge and wanted information.

eliminations.label: jumps to treatment options
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: highlighting available cessation treatments to make quitting seem manageable. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: jumping to treatment is premature - he is not ready, so explore ambivalence first before discussing methods. ✅ Correct if: the patient were ready to quit and choosing a method.

reasoning: Active smoker who is NOT ready to quit but is ambivalent
For a not-ready patient, use motivational interviewing
Explore ambivalence with open-ended, nonjudgmental questions about quitting vs not quitting
Response that does this -> answer C
mechanism: Motivational interviewing is the evidence-based approach for patients NOT ready to change. Its principles are to acknowledge resistance, explore ambivalence, highlight discrepancies between current behavior and long-term goals, and remain nonjudgmental, all to enhance the patient's INTERNAL motivation. The technique is summarized as OARS: Open-ended questions, Affirmations, Reflective listening, and Summarizing. Asking the patient to weigh what he fears about quitting versus not quitting elicits 'change talk' from him, which is more effective than advice, reassurance, or facts imposed by the physician.

difficulty.label: 📚 Pure knowledge gap
difficulty.message: First Aid Behavioral science > motivational interviewing and the transtheoretical (stages of change) model; smoking cessation counseling (the 5 A's). Match technique to the patient's readiness.

difficulty.label: 🧭 Understanding the question
difficulty.message: Gauge readiness first: he is NOT ready. That selects the motivational-interviewing move (explore ambivalence) and rules out advice/education/treatment options regardless of how 'correct' they sound medically.

difficulty.label: 🔤 Vocabulary
difficulty.message: Unsure on motivational interviewing, ambivalence, change talk, OARS, stages of change (precontemplation/contemplation), the 5 A's? Tell me which and I'll explain it.

difficulty.label: 🧲 Pulled by a distractor
difficulty.message: Unsolicited advice (A) feels right because quitting IS the medical goal - but pushing it on a not-ready patient backfires. The MI move explores HIS reasons, so he voices the motivation himself.

difficulty.label: ✍️ Something else
difficulty.message: Bank the rule: not ready to change -> don't preach; ask open-ended, nonjudgmental questions that surface the patient's own pros and cons.

keywords.front: Patient not ready to change a behavior

keywords.back: Explore ambivalence with open-ended, nonjudgmental questions
The 5 A's (Ask, Advise, Assess, Assist, Arrange)

firstAid.detail: Behavioral science > motivational interviewing and stages of change; explore ambivalence to build internal motivation.
firstAid.location: Behavioral science > motivational interviewing and stages of change; explore ambivalence to build internal motivation.

firstAid.detail: Behavioral science > advice vs open-ended questions; nonjudgmental, patient-centered counseling.
firstAid.location: Behavioral science > advice vs open-ended questions; nonjudgmental, patient-centered counseling.

firstAid.detail: Behavioral science > the 5 A's and pharmacotherapy (nicotine replacement, varenicline, bupropion) once the patient is ready.
firstAid.location: Behavioral science > the 5 A's and pharmacotherapy (nicotine replacement, varenicline, bupropion) once the patient is ready.

memoryHook: Not ready? Don't preach - explore. Motivational interviewing asks the patient to name their own pros and cons.
